entries.title: Clickbandit overview - a clickjacking PoC generator
entries.body:
# Burp Clickbandit - overview

**Clickbandit** is a generator, built into Burp, of working PoCs for **clickjacking** (also known as UI redressing) attacks. The tool works entirely in JavaScript inside the browser: you "record" in the browser the actions a victim should perform on a vulnerable site, and Clickbandit assembles a ready HTML exploit from this with a transparent frame and precisely fitted coordinates.

## What clickjacking is (briefly)

Clickjacking is an attack on the interface in which a victim is tricked into clicking not where they think. The attacker places a vulnerable site in a **transparent iframe** and overlays it on top of their bait ("click to get a prize"). The victim sees a harmless button and clicks it, but the click actually lands on an element of the target site hidden under the transparent layer (for example, the "Delete account" or "Confirm transfer" button). Since the victim is already logged into the target site, the action is performed on their behalf.

## Why a separate tool is needed

Assembling a clickjacking PoC by hand is tedious: you need to lay out an iframe, set transparency, and most importantly - **fit the bait's position pixel by pixel** to the right button inside the frame, and on top of that for a sequence of several clicks. Clickbandit removes this routine: you simply click on the target site like an ordinary user, and it computes the coordinates and generates a ready exploit for you.

## How it works in general terms

1. You copy the Clickbandit script from the Burp menu.
2. You open the target site in the browser and paste the script into the developer console - a Clickbandit control panel appears at the top.
3. In **record mode** you go through the victim's scenario: you click on the needed elements of the target site.
4. You switch to **review mode**, where you see the bait overlaid on top of the target and can fit it (transparency, scale, position).
5. You save the result as a standalone **HTML file** - that is the ready PoC.

## Defenses that Clickbandit helps to check

Clickjacking is possible exactly when the site **is allowed to be placed in a frame**. This is hindered by two main defenses on the response side:

* **`X-Frame-Options`** - the old header: `deny` (framing is not allowed at all) or `sameorigin` (only from its own domain). The `allow-from` directive is deprecated and poorly supported by modern browsers.
* **`Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors`** - the modern and more flexible mechanism: `frame-ancestors 'none'` (the analog of `deny`), `'self'` (the analog of `sameorigin`), or a list of allowed domains. Today this is the recommended defense.

Separately it is worth keeping cookies in mind: the **`SameSite`** attribute (`Strict`/`Lax`) partially reduces the risk, because in a cross-site frame cookies may not be sent - but this is only an additional echelon (defense-in-depth), not a replacement for `X-Frame-Options`/`frame-ancestors`. If the target page lacks the needed headers - it is framable, and Clickbandit will work on it.

## Editions

Clickbandit is included in **Burp Suite Professional** (and, consequently, is available in the commercial DAST editions). In descriptions a discrepancy about the Community Edition is sometimes encountered, but count on it primarily as a Professional tool.

## An important security caveat

Clickbandit executes its JavaScript in the context of the open page. Do not run it on sites you do not trust: a malicious script on the page can affect the tool's operation or your browser. Run Clickbandit on targets within your allowed scope.

entries.title: Launching Clickbandit and recording a PoC step by step
entries.body:
# Launching Clickbandit and recording a PoC

This section is a practical end-to-end scenario: from copying the script to a saved HTML exploit. Clickbandit runs not in the Burp window but **in the browser** - Burp only hands out the script.

## Step 1. Copy the Clickbandit script

In Burp's main menu open **Burp menu > Burp Clickbandit** and choose the action to copy the script to the clipboard. Burp will place the Clickbandit JavaScript into the clipboard - there is nothing else to configure in Burp itself.

## Step 2. Open the target and paste the script into the console

1. In the browser go to the page of the target site for which you want to build the PoC (the one with the "interesting" action - deletion, data change, confirmation).
2. Open the browser's developer console (usually **F12** -> the **Console** tab).
3. Paste the copied script into the console and press Enter.

A **Clickbandit control panel** (a noticeable colored bar) with recording controls will appear at the top of the page.

## Step 3. Record the victim's actions (record mode)

1. On the panel click the recording start button (**Start**). The page will reload already inside a frame - Clickbandit is ready to record.
2. **Go through the victim's scenario**: click on those elements of the target site that the victim should click in a real attack. This can be a single click or a chain of several (for example, "open settings" -> "delete account" -> "confirm").
3. Clickbandit records each click and its coordinates on the page.
4. If some click during recording is undesirable to actually perform (it does an irreversible action), the panel has an option to disable the real triggering of clicks (**Disable click actions**) - the clicks are then only recorded as steps but are not really "pressed".
5. When the scenario is recorded, click the finish button (**Finish**) - recording will stop, and Clickbandit will switch to review mode.

## Step 4. Fit the overlay (review mode)

In review mode you see how the bait overlay is placed on the target site, and the recorded sequence of clicks is played back. Here you can polish the PoC:

* **Transparency** - show/hide the lower (target) layer to check whether the bait precisely lands on the right buttons.
* **Scale (zoom)** - enlarge/reduce the overlay so that the coordinates match perfectly.
* **Positioning** - shift the overlay (including with the keyboard arrows) for precise fitting.
* **Reset** - reset the overlay settings to the originals if you "overdid" it.

The goal of this step is to make sure that in the final exploit the visible bait elements precisely cover the right clickable elements of the target site.

## Step 5. Save the PoC

When the overlay is aligned, click save (**Save**). Clickbandit will form a **standalone HTML file** - this is a fully ready exploit: inside are the markup, the styles, and the script that reproduces the recorded sequence of clicks on top of the framed target site.

## How to use the finished HTML

1. Open the saved file in a browser (or host it on your own server and give out a link).
2. If you open it with an active session in the target site and go through the bait the way the victim would, the action will be performed on the target site through the transparent frame. This is the demonstration of the vulnerability.
3. In the generated HTML you can write your own bait text/message (it is editable in the file's code) to make the PoC look more convincing in the report.

## Practice and limitations

* **First find framable pages.** Before Clickbandit it is convenient to run the target through the scanner: Burp Scanner flags the informational finding **Frameable response (potential clickjacking)** where `X-Frame-Options`/`frame-ancestors` are absent. These are the candidates for clickjacking.
* **The target must be framable.** If the page returns `X-Frame-Options: deny` or `CSP frame-ancestors 'none'`, it will not go into a frame, and a PoC cannot be built (without a bypass). Sometimes JS frame-busting can be neutralized with the `sandbox="allow-forms"` attribute on the iframe - this is edited by hand in the generated HTML.
* **Browser support.** Clickbandit works in current browsers; individual engines (for example, Edge) may not be supported - for recording take a proven modern browser.
* **Aim at significant actions.** A PoC has more value when the recorded action is really dangerous: account deletion, e-mail/password change, fund transfer, privilege escalation. For a bug bounty report attach the HTML file itself and indicate in which browser it was tested and which action exactly it demonstrates.
* **Confirm, do not just state the absence of a header.** A working HTML PoC that actually performs the action is far more convincing than a dry "the page has no X-Frame-Options".
